entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
Students use passages from their books to practice dictation skills.

This topic is included in the following course(s): Language Study: Grade 9

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content: Depending on the student, dictation may or may not be necessary at this level.

Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

blocks.text: Read your books for the week in your various subjects, including Essay Voyage.
Narrate after you read and copy down any interesting quotes into your commonplace book!
You will practice many forms of composition over the year. Your books will often serve as the foundation for your assignments.
DICTATION: For one lesson a week, you will prepare to write from dictation a paragraph or more from your readings by visualizing new words and paying close attention to punctuation.

• As you review your students' composition assignments, be sure to read the instructions in the lesson and give feedback and support accordingly.     
• Some students naturally integrate and incorporate ideas from grammar and composition lessons into their narrations and composition assignments, but most assignments ask students to focus on one skill or idea. 
• For editing or revision lessons, hold the student accountable to work through the process and instructions given for editing, but refrain from suggesting too many ideas about how they could expand the essay or add another idea. 
• For assignments that ask a student to focus on a particular concept, ignore spelling or grammar edits, and support your student by noticing what they've done well and drawing out ideas on how to dig deeper.
